text: For those patients undergoing ilioanal pouch failure in whom diversion with ileostomy alone is inadequate or inappropriate, pouch excision is required. The contemporary series of ilioanal pouch excisions at St Mark's has been recently published in Diseases of the Colon and Rectum, including a detailed description of the steps involved. In this video, Professor Sue Clark demonstrates the approach, which has become standardised over time in a high-volume centre.

Ureteric catheters are inserted at the beginning of the procedure to aid in identifying and protecting the ureters. The patient is positioned with a rolled-up towel under the sacrum, covered by a plastic sheet, and the arms are wrapped and secured at the sides.
The surgeon carefully enters the abdomen, performing extensive adhesiolysis to provide sufficient access to the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the bowel just above the pouch and divides it using a linear stapler. The blind end of the pouch is held securely with a Babcock clamp.

The surgeon uses a flexible plastic catheter connected to an enema bag to wash out the pouch. Saline mixed with povidone iodine is instilled into the pouch until full, then drained into a bucket. This process is repeated until the effluent runs clear, with the abdominal operator applying pressure to ensure complete emptying.

The surgeon carefully clears adhesions from the mesentery of the prepouch ileum, identifying the plane between the mesentery and the retroperitoneum. The ureters are visually identified and protected during this process.

The surgeon opens a window between the small bowel mesentery and the retroperitoneum using a clip, passing a tape through the space. Careful inspection and palpation confirm the ureters are positioned behind the tape, while the superior mesenteric artery is in front. The mesentery is further dissected off the retroperitoneum, and traction on the tape lifts the mesentery and superior mesenteric vessels away from the retroperitoneum, enhancing access. The mesentery is divided between clips, ensuring perpendicular division to prevent devascularization of the bowel above the pouch.

The surgeon transfixes the superior mesenteric artery pedicle with proline suture. The bowel proximal to the pouch is packed away in the upper abdomen, and retractors are positioned to enhance access to the pelvis. The pouch is then carefully dissected free down to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon performs dissection in the extrasphincteric plane, using the St Mark's self-retaining retractor for deeper access. An energy device assists in dissecting the indurated pelvic floor. The dissection is completed posteriorly and laterally, allowing the pouch to be delivered for anterior dissection. The pouch and anal canal are examined for integrity, with any residual pouch wall being removed if necessary.

The surgeon closes the subcutaneous tissue in layers using absorbable sutures, followed by interrupted sutures to close the perineal skin. Surgical glue is applied to the skin for protection, and the ureteric catheters are removed. The abdomen is closed, and a suction drain is placed in the pelvis.
We hope this video was informative. Please see the published manuscript for further information on a series of ninety two patients and for further detail on the operative technique.
